sections.title: F Additional Implementation Details
sections.content: Table 14: Implementation details for the training recipes. The main PolicyShiftGuard models use Qwen2.5-VL 3B and 7B backbones. BP-Adapt is the second-stage SFT recipe used for the final models; Think PRM-RL is retained as diagnostic baseline. Recipe Purpose Train Split Objective Key setting RP-SFT Think-SFT BP-Adapt Think PRM-RL Diagnostic RL baseline Stage-1 policy following Diagnostic reasoning SFT Stage-2 boundary adaptation rl rl CE on true XX / false sft sft_think CE on <think>...</think> + final answer LCE + .10Llabel + .20Lpair + .05Lcat format-gated final-answer reward + PRM shaping think-mode rollout; not used as the main recipe 4 epochs, no-think output, LR 105 4 epochs, reasoning target aligned with sft 1 epoch, 1,908 matched pass/block groups, no-think output, LR 106 BP-Adapt consumes the rl split as set of matched boundary pairs. Each group contains the same image and target risk category with one policy that should block and one policy that should pass. The no-think target is intentionally short: blocked samples use true XX, where XX is one valid violated category ID, and passing samples use false. The CE term trains each answer independently, Llabel separates the correct true/false prefix on each prompt, Lcat stabilizes the blocked category token, and Lpair enforces that the same image receives higher unsafe score under the blocking policy than under the passing policy. This is why BP-Adapt differs from ordinary continuation SFT on more boundary data. F.1 Think PRM-RL diagnostic We also tested think-mode RL variant to determine whether explicit reasoning improves policy adaptation. The required output format is <think>...</think> true XX for blocked samples and <think>...</think> false for passing samples. The reward is format-gated first: rfmt = (cid:26)3, 0, the output cannot be parsed into the required format, the output is parseable. If the format gate fails, the rollout receives rfmt and no PRM score is queried. For parseable outputs, the final-answer accuracy reward is answer-gated: 3, wrong safe/unsafe label, 2, +2, correct unsafe label but wrong violated category, correct label and category. rans = The PRM judge then scores only fully correct answers with three process terms: rvis {0.5, +0.5} for image-grounded observations, rpolicy {0.5, +0.5} for using policy clauses present in the prompt, and rlogic {1, +1} for consistency between the reasoning and final answer. The PRMshaped reward is rPRM = 3, 2, 2 + .2rvis + .3rpolicy + .5rlogic, wrong safe/unsafe label, correct unsafe label but wrong violated category, fully correct final answer. Thus PRM is used as reasoning-quality shaping, while exact final-answer correctness remains the gate. Empirically, this diagnostic branch is slower because it must generate and judge rationale, and it gives limited improvement on the deployed decision metrics. In Table 5, the 7B think-mode stage-2 variant reaches 64.6 Avg. F1, while no-think BP-Adapt reaches 76.9 Avg. F1. We therefore use Think PRM-RL as an ablation rather than the main PolicyShiftGuard recipe. For supervised training, online monitoring uses fixed id_test_mini and ood_test_mini callback sets with 100 examples each.

sections.title: G Quality Control and Human Audit
sections.content: G.1 Automatic quality control Quality control combines automatic checks with human auditing. At the annotation level, multi-model majority voting reduces single-model noise and keeps the construction pipeline reproducible. At the dataset level, we enforce split hygiene, image-disjoint major partitions, and validator-backed mini subsets for spot checking. 35 G.2 Human auditing protocol Human auditing has two separate modes. In model-eval, annotators see the image and the policy question, but not the gold answer; this measures human performance on the same policy-conditioned decision task. In data-quality, annotators additionally see the gold answer and judge whether the label is reasonable; this measures label quality rather than human task accuracy. Table 15 reports the current paper-facing audit summary. G.3 Human performance prompt You are evaluating an image safety benchmark example. You will see one image and the full policy bundle that the model receives. Read the policy text carefully before making decision. Judge only the visible image evidence under the provided policy; do not apply your personal preference or generic safety rule. decide whether the image violates any active policy. Task: If the image violates policy, answer true and select one violated risk category ID. If the image does not violate any active policy, answer false. If the image is ambiguous, choose the best answer according to the policy text and briefly note the ambiguity. Do not look at or infer the gold label. G.4 Label-quality audit prompt You are auditing the quality of benchmark label. You will see the image, the policy bundle, and the benchmark gold answer. Your job is not to guess the answer independently, but to decide whether the gold answer is reasonable and auditable under the visible image evidence and the stated policy. qualified or not qualified. Return one of: evidence is missing or misleading, the policy does not support the label, the violated category is wrong, the prompt is malformed, or the case is too ambiguous for reliable benchmark item. issue type from: prompt/format issue, or other, and write short note. wrong label, wrong category, ambiguous policy, image issue, Mark not qualified if the image If not qualified, choose an Table 15: Human evaluation and label-quality audit. Values are percentages. Human Performance measures blind human accuracy without seeing gold labels. Qualified Rate measures whether annotators judge the benchmark labels to be reasonable. Split Human Performance Qualified Rate Adaptive Split Shift Split 88 90 95 95 G.

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.content: The gap between human performance and qualified rate indicates that policy-conditioned moderation is nontrivial even for humans: sample can be well-labeled, yet still be easy to misjudge when small policy clauses change the correct answer. This is precisely the challenge POLICYSHIFTBENCH is designed to expose. G.6 Human audit details Human auditing is used for two purposes: estimating human performance on policy-conditioned decisions and checking benchmark label quality. The annotation interface presents the image and the policy prompt. In the model-eval task, annotators do not see the gold label and must decide whether the image violates the active policy; if they mark it unsafe, they also select the violated category. 36 In the data-quality task, annotators additionally see the gold answer and judge whether the image, policy prompt, and label form reasonable benchmark instance. If sample is marked unreasonable, the interface asks for an issue type such as wrong label, bad policy match, ambiguity, image issue, format issue, or other. Annotators are instructed to judge only the visible image evidence under the provided policy text, not under their personal preference or generic safety taxonomy. They may use optional notes for ambiguous cases. The interface records the selected decision, category or quality flag, optional notes, elapsed annotation time, sample index, and timestamp. It does not collect demographic attributes or sensitive personal information about annotators. Because the benchmark contains potentially disturbing safety content, annotators are warned about sensitive images and may skip examples that they are uncomfortable reviewing. The current audit was conducted as an internal dataset-quality and model-evaluation review rather than public crowdsourcing task. No public crowdworkers were recruited, no external crowdworker compensation was involved, and no demographic or identifying information about reviewers was collected. No formal approval identifier is included in the anonymized submission; if the final venue or institution requires an approval or exemption identifier for this type of internal audit, it should be added in the camera-ready version.

sections.title: H Qualitative Results
sections.content: Figures 615 show policy-flip cases sampled from POLICYSHIFTBENCH. Each case fixes the image and target risk category, then changes only the active policy within that category so that the gold answer flips from false to true. The model outputs are taken from the same evaluation logs used in the main tables. An output is marked correct only when it matches the binary decision and, for unsafe samples, provides an accepted violated-category identifier. These examples illustrate why ordinary image-level safety recognition is insufficient. Several baselines recognize salient risk cues but keep the same decision across both policy branches, or assign the violation to generic category rather than the target policy boundary. In contrast, PolicyShiftGuard usually changes its decision when the policy changes while the image evidence remains fixed. Some images contain multiple visible safety cues; the evaluated flip in each panel isolates the displayed target category and policy pair. 37 Figure 6: Qualitative policy-flip case 1. Nudity and sexual-content example. The same image is permitted under more context-sensitive policy but blocked under stricter family-safe policy. Figure 7: Qualitative policy-flip case 2. Nudity and sexual-content example. The policy change turns pass decision into category-01 block decision. Figure 8: Qualitative policy-flip case 3. Violence, hate, terrorism, and self-harm example. Baselines often over-trigger generic unsafe category or fail to follow the specific violence policy boundary. Figure 9: Qualitative policy-flip case 4. Violence, hate, terrorism, and self-harm example. The policy pair tests whether the model can distinguish an allowed contextual case from blocked category-02 case. 39 Figure 10: Qualitative policy-flip case 5. Regulated goods and substances example. The policy flip depends on whether the active rule allows static or educational presentation versus blocking the regulated-good cue. Figure 11: Qualitative policy-flip case 6. IP, copyright, and brand-safety example from the Shift Split. The same visual evidence is allowed under one policy and blocked under held-out IP/brand policy. Figure 12: Qualitative policy-flip case 7. IP, copyright, and brand-safety example from the Shift Split. Although the image contains other salient visual content, the evaluated flip isolates the displayed category-04 policy boundary. Figure 13: Qualitative policy-flip case 8. Cultural and religious sensitivity example from the Shift Split. The case tests whether the model follows culturally specific policy rather than generic safe/unsafe prior. 41 Figure 14: Qualitative policy-flip case 9. Cultural and religious sensitivity example from the Shift Split. The same image remains acceptable under one cultural policy but is blocked under another policy with stricter taboo constraints. Figure 15: Qualitative policy-flip case 10. Privacy and PII example. sections.title: J Ethics and Responsible Release
sections.content: This work studies safety moderation and therefore necessarily includes images and policies related to sensitive topics such as nudity, violence, regulated goods, identity information, cultural or religious sensitivity, and harmful text. The intended use of the benchmark is to evaluate and improve policyadaptive guardrails. Misuse risks include using the benchmark to probe moderation boundaries, overfitting to fixed policy catalog, or deploying model as sole automatic safety authority without human review. The training and evaluation data necessarily include policy-triggering safety content across the covered risk categories, because the benchmark is designed to evaluate guardrails near policy boundaries. This may expose users to sensitive visual concepts or harmful text. We mitigate this risk by releasing the data with content warnings, acceptable-use terms, compact moderation labels, policy rules, and documentation, and by framing the resource strictly for safety evaluation and guardrail research rather than content generation. We mitigate these risks in three ways. First, policy labels are constructed from explicit rules and voted attributes, so released examples can be audited rather than treated as opaque safety labels. Second, model outputs are restricted to compact moderation decision and violated category, not generative instructions or detailed harmful content. Third, release materials should include the policy catalog, rule code, evaluation scripts, model cards, dataset documentation, content warnings, sourcelicense information for image assets, and clear acceptable-use statement. If raw images cannot be redistributed under their original terms, the release should provide metadata, prompts, labels, and reconstruction instructions that respect the source licenses. Users should treat the benchmark as an evaluation and research resource, not as universal safety policy or substitute for platform-specific legal and policy review. J.1 Data and code availability We provide public releases for both code and data. The code is available at https://github. com/ssmisya/PolicyShiftGuard. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/ datasets/PolicyShiftBench/PolicyShiftBench. The release package includes the benchmark splits, training splits, policy rules, documentation, evaluation utilities, license and terms files, and Croissant metadata. These resources are intended for research evaluation and reproducibility under the stated responsible-use constraints. J.2 Croissant and RAI metadata The dataset release is accompanied by validated Croissant metadata file. The file documents the public data resources, record schemas, checksums, provenance, and the required Responsible AI 43 fields, including dataset limitations, known biases, sensitive-information considerations, intended and out-of-scope use cases, social impact, synthetic-data status, source datasets, and generation activities. J.3 LLM usage disclosure LLMs and MLLMs were used in dataset construction and manuscript preparation. During metadata curation, multimodal annotators predicted category-specific image attributes; these predictions were aggregated by field-level majority vote and were not used as direct policy labels. During think-data construction, an LLM generated concise reasoning traces conditioned on fixed gold labels and policy rules. We also used LLM-based writing assistance for language polishing and formatting. All policy rules, labels, experimental results, analyses, and final manuscript content were reviewed and edited by the authors.
